solution: The standard limit \( \left(1 + \frac{a}{x}\right)^{x} \to e^{a} \) gives \( \lim_{x \to \infty} \left(1 + \frac{[[a]]}{x}\right)^{x} = e^{[[a]]} \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( e \), which drops the exponent \( a \).
This has the wrong sign in the exponent.
This multiplies \( e \) by \( a \) instead of raising \( e \) to the power \( a \).
This doubles the correct value.
This mistakes the limit for the constant \( a \).
This is off by one from the correct limit.

solution: Writing \( \left(1+\frac{[[a]]}{x}\right)^{[[b]]x} = \left[\left(1+\frac{[[a]]}{x}\right)^{x}\right]^{[[b]]} \to (e^{[[a]]})^{[[b]]} = e^{[[a]]\cdot[[b]]} = [[answer]] \), combining the inner coefficient with the outer exponent multiplier.
distractor_rationales: This uses only the inner coefficient \( a \), ignoring the outer multiplier \( b \).
This uses only the outer multiplier \( b \), ignoring the inner coefficient \( a \).
This adds \( a \) and \( b \) instead of multiplying them in the exponent.
This has the wrong sign in the exponent.
This multiplies by \( e \) rather than raising \( e \) to the product \( ab \).
This is off by a factor of \( e \) from the correct value.

solution: Substituting \( x = 1/h \to \infty \) turns this into the standard form \( \left(1+\frac{[[a]]}{x}\right)^{x} \to e^{[[a]]} \), so \( \lim_{h\to 0}(1+[[a]]h)^{1/h} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( e \), which drops the coefficient \( a \).
This has the wrong sign in the exponent.
This multiplies \( e \) by \( a \) instead of raising \( e \) to the power \( a \).
This doubles the correct value.
This mistakes the limit for the coefficient \( a \) itself.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

stem: An account with \( [[p]] \) (in thousands of dollars) earns interest at rate \( [[r]] \) per year, compounded \( n \) times per year. As the compounding frequency \( n \to \infty \) (continuous compounding), the balance after one year approaches \( \lim_{n \to \infty} [[p]]\left(1 + \dfrac{[[r]]}{n}\right)^{n} \). Evaluate this limit (in thousands of dollars).
solution: By the standard limit \( \left(1+\frac{r}{n}\right)^{n} \to e^{r} \), the balance approaches \( [[p]]\cdot e^{[[r]]} = [[answer]] \) thousand dollars.
distractor_rationales: This drops the rate \( r \) from the exponent, using \( e \) alone.
This has the wrong sign in the exponent.
This adds the principal and rate instead of multiplying by \( e^{r} \).
This multiplies the principal and rate directly, ignoring the exponential growth.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
